questions.question: Why are runbooks valuable during incidents?
options: They reduce reliance on memory and guide responders toward safe, repeatable first actions.
They guarantee no incident will need escalation.
They make observability unnecessary.
They replace the need for ownership or paging.
questions.explanation: Runbooks turn partial knowledge into a repeatable response workflow.

questions.question: What is a common weakness in many runbooks?
options: They are too long, stale, or vague to use under stress.
They contain links to dashboards.
They separate safe and dangerous actions.
They mention an owner team.
questions.explanation: If a runbook is not concise and current, responders will ignore it.

questions.question: What should the first section of a practical incident runbook usually help with?
options: Assessing immediate user impact and orienting the responder before deeper diagnosis.
Explaining the full history of the team since the service was created.
Listing every possible command alphabetically.
Describing the company org chart in detail.
questions.explanation: The responder first needs to know whether users are harmed and how serious it is.

questions.question: Why explicitly mark some mitigations as dangerous or expert-only?
options: To prevent responders from taking irreversible actions without the right context or approval.
To discourage anyone from ever using the runbook.
To eliminate the need for communication during incidents.
To ensure all incidents last longer and gather more evidence.
questions.explanation: Clear boundaries reduce accidental blast radius during stressful events.

questions.question: What is a strong escalation rule in a runbook?
options: Escalate when severity, duration, or failed mitigations cross explicit thresholds.
Escalate only when the responder feels tired.
Never escalate because that shows weak ownership.
Escalate only after the incident is fully resolved.
questions.explanation: Explicit conditions prevent both late escalation and unnecessary chaos.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with making a runbook extremely comprehensive?
options: It may contain more detail, but can become too slow to use during the first-response window.
It automatically becomes safer and shorter.
It removes the need for dashboards or alerts.
It guarantees every responder will read the whole document before acting.
questions.explanation: First-response docs need speed and clarity more than encyclopedic completeness.

questions.question: What is a strong sign a runbook is stale?
options: Dashboard links, commands, or mitigation steps no longer match the current service behavior.
The runbook includes a short title.
It mentions safe rollback.
It contains a communication template.
questions.explanation: Broken or outdated operational steps make a runbook actively harmful.

questions.question: What is the strongest answer to 'How do you keep runbooks useful?'
options: Review them after incidents and architectural changes, and keep the first-response path short and tested.
Avoid editing them so responders see stable text for years.
Hide them from new engineers until after their first outage.
Replace them entirely with verbal knowledge transfer.
questions.explanation: Runbooks improve when they are exercised and refreshed from real incidents.
